speaker: Unknown
en: You guys have a billion monthly active users.
zh: 你们有十亿月活跃用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why is it so freaking hard to build a durable, lasting social consumer product?
zh: 为什么构建一个持久、耐用的社交消费者产品是如此该死地难？

speaker: Unknown
en: So much of consumer technology focuses on product market fit.
zh: 消费者技术的大部分都集中在产品市场契合度上。

speaker: Unknown
en: People don't spend nearly enough time thinking about distribution and figuring out distribution.
zh: 人们没有花足够的时间思考和搞清楚分发。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like Snap has always been punching above its weight in terms of just how much new stuff comes out of your team, stories, AR glasses, swipe-based navigation.
zh: 我觉得 Snap 一直以来在团队产出的新东西数量方面总是超水平发挥，Stories、AR 眼镜、基于滑动的导航。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have a very, very small design team that is constantly innovating and creating new things.
zh: 我们有一个非常、非常小的设计团队，它不断创新并创造新事物。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your first day that you join the design team, you present work, you're making things.
zh: 你加入设计团队的第一天，你展示作品，你在做东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you want to have a good idea, you have to have lots of ideas.
zh: 如果你想有一个好主意，你必须有很多主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: People copy you.
zh: 人们抄袭你们。

speaker: Unknown
en: As a human, how does it feel just to see this consistently happen?
zh: 作为一个普通人，看着这种情况持续发生，你感觉如何？

speaker: Unknown
en: 15 years ago, we essentially learned that software is not a moat, which is something that everyone is discovering today with AI.
zh: 15 年前，我们基本上认识到软件不是护城河，这是今天每个人都在随着 AI 发现的一件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: You described this coming year as the crucible moment.
zh: 你把即将到来的一年描述为严峻考验时刻。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a real turning point.
zh: 这是一个真正的转折点。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're in an industry where so much of the conversation is focused on technology.
zh: 我们处在一个行业中，大部分的对话都集中在技术上。

speaker: Unknown
en: Humanity is far more important because humanity dictates how technology is adopted.
zh: 人性要重要得多，因为人性支配了技术如何被采用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Technology leaders think that folks will just blindly adopt new technology as it comes out.
zh: 技术领导人们认为人们只会盲目地采用发布的新技术。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's going to be a huge amount of societal pushback on a lot of the changes that are coming with AI.
zh: 对于即将随着 AI 到来的许多变化，社会上将会有巨大的阻力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Evan Spiegel, CEO and co-founder of Snap.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Evan Spiegel，Snap 的 CEO 和联合创始人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Evan is one of the very few humans in the world who has successfully built and scaled a lasting consumer social app.
zh: Evan 是世界上极少数成功构建并扩展了一个持久的消费者社交应用的人类之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: In the 15 years since Snapchat launched, there are essentially zero social consumer apps that have launched and stuck around.
zh: 在 Snapchat 发布后的 15 年里，基本上没有发布并留存下来的社交消费者应用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Snapchat has over one billion monthly active users, is generating over $6 billion a year in revenue.
zh: Snapchat 拥有超过十亿月活跃用户，每年产生超过 60 亿美元的收入。

speaker: Unknown
en: People post over eight billion AR lens photos a day on Snapchat.
zh: 人们每天在 Snapchat 上发布超过 80 亿个 AR 镜头照片。

speaker: Unknown
en: And over the years, Evan and his team have invented the concept of stories.
zh: 这些年来，Evan 和他的团队发明了 Stories 的概念。

speaker: Unknown
en: They had the first AR glasses product in the market.
zh: 他们拥有市场上第一个 AR 眼镜产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: They invented swipe face navigation, the camera being primary, and also back in the day, face swapping, making people look older, and so many of the things that are just copied throughout the entire industry.
zh: 他们发明了滑动面部导航、以相机为主，还有回到过去，面部交换、让人看起来变老，以及太多被整个行业抄袭的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: If this is truly the golden age of consumer products, like many people say AI is going to enable, there's a lot that we can learn about how Evan and his team think and operate and are able to continue innovating.
zh: 如果这真的是消费者产品的黄金时代，就像很多人说 AI 将要实现的那样，我们有太多可以向 Evan 和他的团队如何思考和运作以及能够继续创新学习的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is a rare podcast interview with Evan and we cover a lot of ground.
zh: 这是一个与 Evan 的难得的播客采访，我们涵盖了很多领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into it, don't forget to check out Lenny'sproductpass.com for a year free of the hottest and most well-crafted AI products in the world available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 在进入正题之前，别忘了去查看 Lenny'sproductpass.com，免费获取一年全球最热门和精心制作的 AI 产品，专供 Lenny 的通讯订阅者。

speaker: Unknown
en: With that, I bring you Evan Spiegel.
zh: 话虽如此，我为您带来 Evan Spiegel。

speaker: Unknown
en: Evan, thank you so much for being here.
zh: Evan，非常感谢你能来这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: Welcome to the podcast.
zh: 欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thank you so much.
zh: 非常感谢。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thanks for having me.
zh: 谢谢你的邀请。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm looking forward to it.
zh: 我很期待。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to start with just trying to understand and help people understand why is it so freaking hard to build a durable, lasting consumer product and in particular a social consumer product.
zh: 我想首先尝试理解并帮助大家理解为什么构建一个持久、耐用的消费者产品是如此该死地难，特别是社交消费者产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just to give people this context, I don't think people realize this, but since you launched 15 years ago, there's basically TikTok, which is not really social.
zh: 为了给大家一些背景，我不认为大家意识到了这一点，但自从你们 15 年前发布以来，基本上只有 TikTok，它并不是真正的社交产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's more of like a media platform might say.
zh: 可能更像是一个媒体平台，我可能会说。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then maybe threads, which I think is cheating because it just sits on top of Instagram.
zh: 然后可能是 threads，我认为这不算数，因为它只是建立在 Instagram 之上。

speaker: Unknown
en: Basically, nothing else has worked other than Snapchat 15 years ago.
zh: 基本上，除了 15 年前的 Snapchat 之外，没有其他成功的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in spite of everyone just ... Everyone innately just wants to build a consumer product, social app.
zh: 尽管每个人都……每个人天生就想构建一个消费者产品，社交应用。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's just like where everyone first goes.
zh: 这就像每个人首先去的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everybody fails.
zh: 每个人都失败了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Nothing works.
zh: 什么都不管用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why is it so hard?
zh: 为什么这么难？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you think people don't get?
zh: 你觉得大家不懂什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think it's really interesting in terms of the examples that you just shared of TikTok and threads, because you just shared two examples of people who figured out distribution.
zh: 嗯，我觉得就你刚才分享的 TikTok 和 threads 的例子来说，这真的很有趣，因为你刚才分享了两个搞清楚分发的人的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that that's actually one of the hardest things to figure out in consumer technology today.
zh: 我认为这实际上是今天消费者技术中最难搞清楚的事情之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were so fortunate when we created Snapchat, the mobile phone and the app store were just getting started.
zh: 当我们创建 Snapchat 时，我们非常幸运，手机和应用商店才刚刚起步。

speaker: Unknown
en: So people were downloading lots of new apps all the time.
zh: 所以人们当时一直在下载很多新应用。

speaker: Unknown
en: They were really excited about trying new services.
zh: 他们真的很兴奋尝试新服务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Instagram, I think it started a year before Snapchat or something like that.
zh: Instagram，我想它比 Snapchat 早一年开始或类似的情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there was a real appetite to try new apps and new services.
zh: 所以当时确实有强烈的欲望去尝试新的应用和新的服务。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's not the case today.
zh: 而今天的情况并非如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a lot harder to get distribution for new ideas and new services.
zh: 为新想法和新服务获得分发变得更难了。

speaker: Unknown
en: People aren't downloading as many apps now as they used to.
zh: 人们现在下载的应用不像以前那么多了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And both TikTok and Threads figured out distribution, which is why I think there are more recent examples of success.
zh: 而且 TikTok 和 Threads 都搞定了分发，这就是我认为有更多最近的成功案例的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: TikTok did it with money, which I actually thought was really innovative.
zh: TikTok 用钱做到了这一点，这实际上我觉得非常有创新性。

speaker: Unknown
en: They spent billions of dollars subsidizing both sides of their video marketplace, acquiring customers to watch videos and then paying creators to make videos.
zh: 他们花费数十亿美元补贴其视频市场的双方，既获取客户观看视频，又支付给创作者制作视频。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so they were able to bootstrap their ecosystem.
zh: 因此他们能够引导他们的生态系统。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think with Threads, obviously, they were able to leverage the amazing distribution that Meta has across all of their other products.
zh: 我认为对于 Threads 来说，显然他们能够利用 Meta 在其所有其他产品中拥有的惊人分发能力。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think so much of consumer technology focuses on, am I building the right product?
zh: 所以我认为很多消费技术都专注于，我在构建正确的产品吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do I have product market fit?
zh: 我有产品市场契合度吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Have I built something that's really going to resonate with customers that they're really going to want to use all the time?
zh: 我构建的东西是否真的能引起客户的共鸣，让他们真的想一直使用？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think people don't spend nearly enough time thinking about distribution and figuring out distribution.
zh: 我认为人们没有花足够的时间去思考分发并弄清楚分发。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that seems to me to be a huge differentiator.
zh: 在我看来，这似乎是一个巨大的差异化因素。

speaker: Unknown
en: So as I reflect on the early days of building Snapchat, the thing that we figured out in terms of distribution, especially when it comes to social, was that back then people believed that there were network effects and social networks that meant that the more people you had using the service, the stickier that it is.
zh: 所以当我反思构建 Snapchat 的早期时，我们在分发方面弄清楚的事情，特别是当涉及到社交时，是那时人们相信存在网络效应和社交网络，这意味着使用服务的人越多，它就越有粘性。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there was no way to beat the biggest networks because those were the most valuable.
zh: 所以没有办法打败最大的网络，因为那些是最有价值的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what Snapchat figured out that was, I think, different was that despite the fact that there were much bigger networks that connected more people, what really mattered was connecting you to the right people.
zh: 而 Snapchat 发现的不同之处在于，我认为，尽管有连接更多人的更大的网络，但真正重要的是将你连接到合适的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if you could just connect someone, not to all their friends, but to their best friend, to their partner, to their spouse, the people that they cared most about in the world, that's where the majority of the value is in the network.
zh: 所以如果你能连接一个人，不是连接到他们所有的朋友，而是连接到他们最好的朋友、他们的伴侣、他们的配偶、他们在世界上最关心的人，这就是网络中大部分价值所在的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that's what really allowed us to grow in those early days.
zh: 这就是让我们能够在早期真正成长的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had the benefit of the app store and mobile distribution, but we also had a different way of growing our service, which wasn't about having the most friends.
zh: 我们拥有应用商店和移动分发的优势，但我们也有一种不同的服务增长方式，这不是关于拥有最多的朋友。

speaker: Unknown
en: It was about having your close friends.
zh: 而是关于拥有你的亲密朋友。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is so interesting.
zh: 这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's something that I've been thinking a lot about actually, this idea of distribution, because of AI.
zh: 实际上这是我一直在思考很多的事情，关于分发的这个想法，因为 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about just where AI has come into the market, it started originally just like helping people auto complete code.
zh: 如果你考虑一下 AI 刚进入市场时的位置，它最初只是帮助人们自动完成代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's something I've been talking about a lot on this podcast.
zh: 这是我在这个播客上经常谈论的一件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's like, okay, auto-complete code.
zh: 所以就像，好吧，自动完成代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, now it's writing all our code.
zh: 好的，现在它正在编写我们所有的代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now it's reviewing our code and now it's testing itself and now it's going to help us come up with ideas.
zh: 现在它正在审查我们的代码，现在它正在测试自己，现在它将帮助我们提出想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's like starting in the middle of the product development process and expanding out.
zh: 所以就像从产品开发过程的中间开始并向外扩展。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I feel like it will be very good at coming up with great ideas and developing strategy.
zh: 我觉得它将非常擅长提出伟大的想法和发展战略。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's going up, I don't know, the funnel or the timeline of building a product.
zh: 它正在上升，我不知道，漏斗或构建产品的时间线。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I feel like distribution is where it ends up being, what ends up being the new moat and the new biggest challenge because AI is not going to really help you there.
zh: 我觉得分发最终会成为它结束的地方，最终成为新的护城河和新的最大挑战，因为 AI 在那里真的帮不了你。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's really interesting that that's even more so true for consumer products.
zh: 所以非常有趣的是，对于消费产品来说，情况更是如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: No, I think that's absolutely the case.
zh: 不，我认为情况绝对是这样的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the only thing I would say is the most exciting times in technology are when there are new platforms that get created.
zh: 我唯一想说的是，科技中最令人兴奋的时刻是当有新平台被创建的时候。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that tends to be where a lot of the value comes from.
zh: 这往往是很多价值来源的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: You think about a lot of the big consumer technology companies today, they were really born out of mobile, whether it's Uber, Snapchat, some of those early generation companies that really got distribution and built themselves on mobile.
zh: 你想想今天许多大型消费技术公司，它们真的诞生于移动时代，无论是 Uber、Snapchat，还是那些真正在移动端获得分销并建立自己的早期一代公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think as we look forward to these next generation form factors, things like glasses, there's going to be a whole new set of opportunities and a whole new surface for people to build generational consumer companies.
zh: 所以我认为当我们展望这些下一代形态因素时，比如眼镜之类的东西，将会有一套全新的机会和全新的表面供人们建立代际消费公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm really excited about that.
zh: 我对此感到非常兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: Especially if they have distribution already.
zh: 特别是如果他们已经有分发渠道。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm just worried for startups.
zh: 我只是为初创公司担心。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's going to be very hard to get distribution just like there's so much launching every day, so much to pay attention to.
zh: 获得分发将非常困难，就像每天都有这么多东西发布，有这么多东西值得关注。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it just feels very hard for a startup to get any attention against existing incumbents.
zh: 对于初创公司来说，对抗现有的在位者获得任何注意力感觉非常困难。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that has always been really interesting to me about Snapchat is it feels like you guys have just innovated so much over the years.
zh: 关于 Snapchat，一直以来有一件事对我来说真的很有趣，那就是感觉你们这些年来真的创新了很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: You guys basically just invent all the things.
zh: 你们基本上发明了所有的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: People copy you, benefit greatly from it, and it's just kind of wild to watch.
zh: 人们抄袭你们，从中获益匪浅，而且看起来真的有点狂野。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just like a few examples that come to mind, stories you guys created.
zh: 就想到几个例子，Stories 是你们创造的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You were very early on AR glasses.
zh: 你们很早就进入了 AR 眼镜领域。

speaker: Unknown
en: You guys launched spectacles before Meta got into this stuff.
zh: 你们在 Meta 涉足这个领域之前就发布了 Spectacles。

speaker: Unknown
en: Swipe-based navigation.
zh: 基于滑动的导航。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think you guys started the idea of holding to do a video versus a photo.
zh: 我想是你们开启了按住拍摄视频而不是照片的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's probably a bunch.
zh: 可能还有很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: First of all, what's the next thing someone's going to steal, do you think?
zh: 首先，你认为下一件别人会偷走的东西是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh gosh.
zh: 天哪。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I just saw Instagram plus launch, so I think that's the latest rip.
zh: 嗯，我刚看到 Instagram plus 发布，所以我认为这是最新的抄袭。

speaker: Unknown
en: Which is a subscription.
zh: 这是一个订阅服务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think we just hit 25 million subscribers on Snapchat plus more than a billion revenue run rate.
zh: 是的，我想我们刚刚在 Snapchat plus 上达到了 2500 万订阅者，超过 10 亿美元的营收运行率。

speaker: Unknown
en: So was I think probably enough to get Meta's attention that it's a good time to copy.
zh: 所以我想这足以引起 Meta 的注意，是抄袭的好时机了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the plus, he's just even taking the plus as the name.
zh: 而且 plus，他甚至直接把 plus 作为名字拿走了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh my God.
zh: 我的天。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let me just ask you, as a human, how does it feel just to see this consistently happen to you?
zh: 我只想问问你，作为一个人，看到这种情况持续发生在你身上感觉如何？

speaker: Unknown
en: You guys come up with something awesome, someone rips it off, benefits hugely from it, and you just have to keep doing that again and again.
zh: 你们想出了很棒的东西，有人抄袭了它，从中获得巨大利益，而你们只是不得不一次又一次地继续这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's certainly better than making stuff that people don't want to copy.
zh: 这当然比做出人们不想抄袭的东西要好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think in many ways it's a blessing that we've been able to continue to innovate to make new things that really resonate with folks and that ultimately people want to end up copying from Snap.
zh: 所以我认为在很多方面这是一种福分，我们已经能够继续创新，制作出真正能引起人们共鸣的新东西，最终人们想要从 Snap 复制。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think it certainly beats the alternative, but it does mean that we have to be really thoughtful about our strategy.
zh: 所以我认为这当然比替代方案要好，但这确实意味着我们必须对我们的策略非常深思熟虑。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think one of the things, it's been an interesting time for me to reflect a lot on our strategy over the last 15 years, because 15 years ago, we essentially learned that software is not a moat, which is something that everyone is discovering today with AI.
zh: 所以我认为有一件事，这段时间对我来说很有趣，就是反思我们在过去 15 年的策略，因为 15 年前，我们基本上得知软件不是护城河，这是今天每个人都在通过 AI 发现的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: But 15 years ago, because all the software features that we could create were so easily cloned by our competitors, we started to think about how to build a more durable business, how to build a business that had bigger and more effective modes.
zh: 但是 15 年前，因为我们能创建的所有软件功能都很容易被竞争对手克隆，我们开始思考如何建立更持久的业务，如何建立拥有更大更有效的护城河的业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the first things we did was focus a lot on building ecosystems, whether those are the relationships between creators and Snap-chatters, whether that's the platform we built around augmented reality where developers have built millions of these lenses.
zh: 我们做的第一件事就是专注于构建生态系统，无论是创作者和 Snap 聊天用户之间的关系，还是我们在增强现实周围建立的平台，开发者已经构建了数百万个这样的滤镜。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's very easy to copy software features.
zh: 所以复制软件功能很容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's very hard to copy or to replicate a full ecosystem or a platform.
zh: 复制或复制完整的生态系统或平台是非常困难的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we realized that the more that we could build a platform around our business, the more that we could build an ecosystem with creators and developers, the harder it would be for folks to just copy that.
zh: 所以我们意识到，我们围绕业务构建的平台越多，我们与开发者和开发者构建的生态系统越多，人们就越难复制它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it also informed a lot of our thinking about investing in other places that are really hard to copy, including hardware, where it's really, really challenging to copy our fully vertically integrated stack around augmented reality.
zh: 这也影响了很多我们的思考，即投资于其他真正难以复制的地方，包括硬件，在那里复制我们围绕增强现实的完全垂直整合堆栈真的非常非常具有挑战性。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'm certain there's a lot more to talk about there, but I think we learned early on that software's easy to copy, and so it's really important to build more durable modes.
zh: 所以我相信那里还有很多要谈的，但我认为我们很早就了解到软件很容易复制，所以构建更持久的护城河非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's really interesting hearing this from you.
zh: 从你这里听到这个真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was going to get to this later, but we can talk about this now, this big investment you've been putting into hardware.
zh: 我本来打算稍后再谈这个，但我们可以现在就谈，你们在硬件上投入的巨资。

speaker: Unknown
en: You would think network effects would be the moat.
zh: 你会认为网络效应就是护城河。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's what everybody's always trying to get to because that's classically a great moat.
zh: 这就是每个人总是试图达到的目标，因为那传统上是一个伟大的护城河。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you saying that that's maybe not even as great a moat or that's just not enough and then you need something more like say hardware?
zh: 你是说那可能甚至不是那么好的护城河，或者那还不够，然后你需要更多的东西，比如硬件？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think network effects are certainly an important piece of the puzzle for sure, but I don't think they're enough when it comes to these sorts of software cloning.
zh: 我认为网络效应肯定是谜题的重要组成部分，但当涉及到这些类型的软件克隆时，我不认为它们足够了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so despite the fact that we've got a ton of patents and we protected all these ideas, the way that it works in software is that it's quite easy for folks to copy or iterate on top of these ideas.
zh: 所以尽管我们拥有大量的专利并且我们保护了所有这些想法，软件中的工作方式是人们很容易复制或在这些想法之上进行迭代。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think in addition to network effects, it's really important to try to build businesses that are more defensible by truly building out a platform that can support relationships between creators, developers, and the community, because that in and of itself we found is incredibly difficult to copy.
zh: 所以我认为除了网络效应，试图通过真正构建一个能够支持创作者、开发者和社区之间关系的平台来建立更具防御性的业务非常重要，因为我们发现这本身就极其难以复制。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so interesting.
zh: 太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you think about it, you're working on two of the hardest types of businesses, a consumer social platform and hardware.
zh: 如果你想想看，你正在从事两种最难类型的业务，一个消费者社交平台和硬件。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I love that you're doing that.
zh: 我很喜欢你们这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: And this hardware investment, you've been at it for a long time.
zh: 而且这种硬件投资，你们已经坚持了很长时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: You had spectacles, you had drones.
zh: 你们有了 Spectacles，你们有了无人机。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think people are remembering yet this drone that could take photos of you.
zh: 我认为人们还没有记得那架可以给你拍照的无人机。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then you have specs coming out.
zh: 然后你们有 Specs 发布。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it specs or spectacles the name of what's coming out today, by the way?
zh: 顺便问一下，今天发布的东西叫 Specs 还是 Spectacles？

speaker: Unknown
en: Specs is coming out this year.
zh: Specs 今年发布。

speaker: Unknown
en: Specs.
zh: Specs。

speaker: Evan Spiegel
en: Yep.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, cool.
zh: 好的，酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to talk about it, but just maybe you've already answered it, why this is something you're so passionate about, but why this kind of ongoing investment in hardware?
zh: 我们要谈谈这个，但也可能你已经回答了，为什么这是你如此热衷的事情，以及为什么这种对硬件的持续投资？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think to take a huge step back, I love computers.
zh: 嗯，我认为从宏观角度来看，我热爱计算机。

speaker: Unknown
en: I grew up using computers all the time.
zh: 我从小就一直使用计算机。

speaker: Unknown
en: I built my own computer when I was in middle school.
zh: 我在中学的时候自己组装了一台计算机。

speaker: Unknown
en: I fell in love with graphic design and would spend every lunch period in the computer lab.
zh: 我爱上了平面设计，会把每个午餐时间都花在计算机实验室里。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think one of the things that I noticed about computers and I think today with mobile phones is that in many ways they isolate us from one another.
zh: 我认为我注意到计算机的一件事，以及今天关于手机的是，它们在很多方面将我们彼此隔离开来。

speaker: Unknown
en: They actually take us out of our social interactions.
zh: 它们实际上让我们脱离了社交互动。

speaker: Unknown
en: They took me off the playground at recess and put me into the computer lab.
zh: 它们让我在课间休息时离开了操场，进入了计算机实验室。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or if I look at our 15-year-old today and his friends, sometimes when they're hanging out, they're all looking down at their phones, even though they're all together.
zh: 或者如果我看看我们今天 15 岁的孩子和他的朋友们，有时当他们聚在一起时，他们都在低头看手机，尽管他们都在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think there's just such a big opportunity to build technology that actually brings us together, that keeps us grounded in the real world rather than removing us from it.
zh: 所以我认为构建一种真正将我们聚集在一起的技术有着巨大的机会，这种技术让我们扎根于现实世界，而不是将我们从中剥离。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's something that we realized early on with Snapchat.
zh: 这是我们很早以前就在 Snapchat 上意识到的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were innovating, building lenses and augmented reality, but ultimately we saw that people were using AR on these tiny little phone screens that required you to use your thumbs to interact.
zh: 我们在创新，构建镜头和增强现实，但最终我们看到人们在这些微小的手机屏幕上使用 AR，需要你使用拇指来进行交互。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it was almost like interacting with the world through a keyhole rather than actually staying grounded and being able to interact together with friends.
zh: 所以这几乎就像是通过锁孔与世界互动，而不是真正能够保持立足点并与朋友一起互动。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it just seems so obvious that as computers play a bigger and bigger role in our lives, I think today people spend seven or eight hours a day on average on screens that something has got to change to make this technology fit better into our lives.
zh: 所以很明显，随着计算机在我们要生活中扮演越来越重要的角色，我认为今天人们平均每天在屏幕上花费七八个小时，有些事情必须改变，以使这项技术更适合我们的生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: We get enormous value from computers, but at the same time, I think the cost is quite meaningful and I think they need to evolve.
zh: 我们从计算机那里获得了巨大的价值，但同时，我认为代价也是相当可观的，我认为它们需要进化。

speaker: Unknown
en: So with the early version of Spectacles, I guess that was back in 2014 or something like that, the initial idea was just to get the camera off the phone out of your pocket and allow you to more easily take snaps out in the world.
zh: 所以对于 Spectacles 的早期版本，我想那是 2014 年左右，最初的构想只是把摄像头从手机上拿出来，从你的口袋里拿出来，让你更容易在世界各地拍摄 Snap。

speaker: Unknown
en: But we've then sort of built a roadmap from there where we added a second camera to get depth.
zh: 但随后我们从那里制定了一个路线图，添加了第二个摄像头来获取深度。

speaker: Unknown
en: We added a display to be able to overlay digital objects into the world and help you interact with them.
zh: 我们添加了一个显示器，以便能够将数字对象叠加到世界中并帮助你与它们交互。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then with the last generation in 2024, we released our operating system, which now allows developers to build full featured software inside of specs in preparation for the consumer launch.
zh: 然后在 2024 年的上一代产品中，我们发布了我们的操作系统，它现在允许开发者在 specs 内构建功能齐全的软件，为消费者发布做准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I don't know, I think the time is right for a new type of computer.
zh: 所以我不知道，我认为时机已经成熟，可以出现一种新型计算机了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think people are frustrated with the cost of using phones and computers today.
zh: 我认为人们今天对使用手机和计算机的代价感到沮丧。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're kind of all hunched over like gremlins and we can build a new computer.
zh: 他们都像小妖精一样佝偻着身子，我们可以构建一台新计算机。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have built a new computer that brings people outside, that helps them connect together with their friends, that frees them up to actually use their hands to interact with the world.
zh: 我们已经构建了一台新计算机，它把人们带到户外，帮助他们与朋友联系在一起，让他们腾出手来与世界互动。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that to me is really exciting.
zh: 这对我来说真的很令人兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's a whole new chapter.
zh: 我认为这是一个全新的篇章。

speaker: Unknown
en: And gosh, I mean, what?
zh: 天哪，我是说，什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's been 20 years or something since the iPhone.
zh: 距离 iPhone 已经 20 年左右了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's about time for something new, I think.
zh: 所以我认为是时候有些新东西了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I got an early preview of the specs and it's extremely cool.
zh: 所以我提前预览了 specs，它非常酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: I learned how to be an air bender.
zh: 我学会了如何成为一名御气师。

speaker: Unknown
en: I put plants all over my house drawing all over the place and there's multiplayer features, which is very cool, which I think is part of the idea, is you're connecting with people versus sitting in a VR environment on your own.
zh: 我在我家里到处放植物，到处画画，而且有多人游戏功能，这非常酷，我认为这是想法的一部分，就是你与人连接，而不是独自坐在 VR 环境中。

speaker: Unknown
en: I worry about being connected all the time.
zh: 我担心一直保持连接。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I imagine that's like a new thing we have to figure out.
zh: 所以我想这是我们需要弄清楚的一件新事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we not just get hooked on just watching things onto the side instead of looking at someone with?
zh: 我们如何才能不只是习惯于在一旁看东西，而不是看着某人？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you have any thoughts there?
zh: 你对此有什么想法吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Just is there a solution to that other than just like social norms?
zh: 除了社会规范之外，是否有解决方案？

speaker: Unknown
en: I do think social norms will play a big role, but I also think one of the things people experience with AR, specs, unlike these heads up display glasses, they don't just put an annoying little screen that's stuck to your face on the glasses.
zh: 我确实认为社会规范将发挥重要作用，但我也认为人们在使用 AR、specs 时体验到的与这些抬头显示眼镜不同的是，它们不仅仅是在眼镜上放一个粘在你脸上的恼人小屏幕。

speaker: Unknown
en: They actually anchor content in the world, which is really different.
zh: 它们实际上将内容锚定在世界中，这真的很不同。

speaker: Unknown
en: They put that content directly in your field of view rather than requiring you to look down at some little screen or get some text alert or notification.
zh: 它们将内容直接放在你的视野中，而不需要你低头看某个小屏幕或收到某些文本警报或通知。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think, frankly, I don't think those use cases are very compelling.
zh: 所以我认为，坦率地说，我认为这些用例并不是很有吸引力。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think receiving phone notifications on your face is a valuable proposition for most folks.
zh: 我认为在脸上接收手机通知对大多数人来说不是一个有价值的提议。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I don't think we necessarily have to worry about that because I think most people don't want that.
zh: 所以我认为我们不必为此担心，因为我认为大多数人不想要那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think what is going to be really important with specs is showing people all of the incredible new ways they can hang out and connect together with their friends wearing specs, but I don't think it'll be disruptive or notification based or something like that.
zh: 我认为对 specs 来说真正重要的是要向人们展示他们可以戴着 specs 与朋友闲逛和联系的所有令人难以置信的新方式，但我认为它不会具有破坏性或基于通知或类似的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the things that I really dislike about the heads-up display glasses is when you're looking at the little heads up display in the corner, you're actually looking at your friend's crotch while you're talking to them if you check the heads up display.
zh: 我非常讨厌抬头显示眼镜的一件事是，当你看角落里的小抬头显示器时，如果你检查抬头显示器，你实际上是在看你朋友的裤裆，而你正在和他们交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the position is just very strange.
zh: 所以这个立场非常奇怪。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't think that that's something that really is resonating with customers, nor do I think that's something that's going to become like a widespread behavior.
zh: 我不认为这真的能引起客户的共鸣，我也不认为这会成为一种普遍的行为。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to go back to this idea of how to innovate.
zh: 我想回到关于如何创新这个想法上。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like Snap has always been punching above its weight in terms of just how much new stuff comes out of your team, how many inventions happen considering the size of the company and the history.
zh: 我觉得考虑到公司的规模和历史，Snap 一直以超出其身手的表现输出新东西和发明的数量。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you find needs to be true for innovation to happen?
zh: 你认为要让创新发生需要具备什么条件？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you do to allow for all these great ideas to emerge?
zh: 你做了什么来让所有这些伟大的想法涌现出来？

speaker: Unknown
en: The best sort of academic overview or summary I think comes from a guy, Safi Bahcall, who wrote a book called Loon Shots.
zh: 我认为最好的学术概览或总结来自一个叫 Safi Bahcall 的人，他写了一本书叫《Loon Shots》。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really worth reading.
zh: 这本书真的值得一读。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's all about the innovation process.
zh: 它是关于创新过程的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And actually when I read it, I was like, "Oh my gosh, that kind of explains a lot of how we think about innovation at Snap."
zh: 实际上当我读它的时候，我想，“天哪，这有点解释了我们在 Snap 思考创新的很多方式。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think to summarize the academic version, we can talk about what we've developed and built at Snap, but to summarize, essentially his findings are that basically if you want to deliver a product at scale, you usually need quite a large organization.
zh: 我想总结一下学术版本，我们可以谈谈我们在 Snap 开发和构建的东西，但总结来说，基本上他的发现是，如果你想大规模交付产品，通常需要一个相当大的组织。

speaker: Unknown
en: A large organization needs hierarchy and structure, a lot of operational rigor.
zh: 大型组织需要层级和结构，需要大量的运营严谨性。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as soon as you have an organization that's very large and very structured like that, people become very focused on getting a promotion, getting into the next step of the hierarchy.
zh: 一旦你拥有了一个非常大、非常有条理的组织，人们就会非常专注于获得晋升，进入层级中的下一步。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that means that all of a sudden they become a lot more risk averse.
zh: 这意味着他们突然变得更加厌恶风险。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're not really as willing to try new things.
zh: 他们真的不太愿意尝试新事物。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're very focused on achieving the goals that have sort of been set for them in this operational hierarchy, and that makes it very, very challenging to innovate.
zh: 他们非常专注于实现在这个运营层级中为他们设定的目标，这使得创新变得非常非常具有挑战性。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now on the flip side, structures that are really healthy and constructive for innovation typically are very, very flat.
zh: 现在另一方面，对创新真正健康和建设性的结构通常是非常非常扁平的。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's the same for our design team, for example, where there's no one that has a fancier title or a role.
zh: 我们的设计团队也是如此，例如，那里没有人拥有更花哨的头衔或角色。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a lot of flexibility in terms of the ability to try new things and fail quickly and put up crazy ideas.
zh: 在尝试新事物和快速失败以及提出疯狂想法的能力方面有很大的灵活性。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what he basically finds is that the companies that are very successful actually have both types of organizations inside their company and that the leaders of the organization are the ones who are responsible for creating a healthy functioning relationship between the two types of organizations.
zh: 他基本上发现的是，非常成功的公司实际上在公司内部拥有这两种类型的组织，而组织领导者是负责在两种类型的组织之间建立健康功能关系的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because what ends up happening in a lot of companies is that the small innovative team becomes critical of the large organization, oh, they're so bureaucratic and slow, they don't innovate, things like that.
zh: 因为在许多公司最终发生的是，小型创新团队开始批评大型组织，噢，他们太官僚、太慢了，他们不创新，诸如此类。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the larger organization is like, "Oh, what are these Jokers doing?"
zh: 然后大型组织会说，“噢，这些小丑在干什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Just innovating, they're not really driving the business, they're not supporting our customers.
zh: 只是在搞创新，他们并没有真正推动业务，也没有支持我们的客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so a lot of tension actually arises from the different organizational structures.
zh: 所以实际上很多紧张关系源于不同的组织结构。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so the leadership responsibility, and I think Safi does a really good job looking throughout history, what leaders have cultivated really constructive and powerful relationships between the more innovative or flat parts of the organization and the more structured and hierarchical parts of the organization, how you create that mutual respect, how you create that dialogue between those parts of the organization is so important.
zh: 所以领导的责任，我认为 Safi 在纵观历史方面做得非常好，哪些领导者真正培养了一种建设性和强大的关系，在更具创新性或扁平的组织部分与更具结构化和层级的组织部分之间，你如何创造那种相互尊重，你如何创造组织各部分之间的对话是如此重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so when I read that, I was like, wow, that actually sounds quite similar to what we have at Snap.
zh: 所以当我读到那个时，我想，哇，这实际上听起来和我们在 Snap 的情况非常相似。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have to run a large public company, serve almost a billion customers.
zh: 我们必须经营一家大型上市公司，服务近十亿客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: That means our service needs to be really reliable and it means that we need to operate really, really effectively to serve our customers.
zh: 这意味着我们的服务需要真正可靠，也意味着我们需要非常非常有效地运营来服务我们的客户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And at the same time, we have a very, very small ...
zh: 同时，我们有一个非常非常小的……

speaker: Unknown
en: It sort of oscillates between like nine and 12 people design team that is constantly innovating and creating new things.
zh: 它在 9 到 12 人的设计团队之间波动，不断进行创新和创造新事物。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the beauty of what I love about Snap is that the dialogue between those two teams is where a lot of that innovation happens because a lot of our engineers working to serve our customers or working on reliability have a bunch of great ideas and so do our designers.
zh: 我认为我对 Snap 喜爱的美妙之处在于，这两个团队之间的对话是许多创新发生的地方，因为我们许多致力于服务客户或致力于可靠性的工程师有很多伟大的想法，我们的设计师也是如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as long as they're in dialogue, really great things can happen.
zh: 只要他们保持对话，真正美好的事情就会发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's someone, I forget who this was, but they described this approach as the fast thinking and slow thinking part of the organization, kind of like Kahneman's System 1, System 2 approach.
zh: 有人，我忘了这是谁，但他们把这种方法描述为组织的快思考和慢思考部分，有点像 Kahneman 的系统 1，系统 2 方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's interesting, the fast moving, let's say.
zh: 很有趣，快速移动的，比如说。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you kind of call this innovation team or do you have a label for this like, let's come up with the new ideas part of the org?
zh: 你怎么称呼这个创新团队，或者你有没有给这部分想出新想法的组织贴个标签？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think it's really important to acknowledge that new ideas come from everywhere and we want that.
zh: 嗯，我认为承认新想法来自任何地方并且我们希望如此是非常重要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think the design team in particular, it's really important, and we call it the design team, it's really important that they have a flat non-hierarchical structure where new ideas can come from anywhere.
zh: 但我认为特别是设计团队，它非常重要，我们称之为设计团队，他们拥有扁平的非层级结构，新想法可以来自任何地方，这非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, I think that that is absolutely critical.
zh: 我认为这绝对是至关重要的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So in terms of how you operationalize this, is that the key to the innovation you've seen as this specific small design team who can work?
zh: 所以就你如何将此落地而言，你所看到的创新的关键就是这个特定的小型设计团队可以工作吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Talk about just how you actually operationalize this sort of way of working.
zh: 谈谈你是如何真正将这种工作方式落地的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, I think it's an important part of it, but a lot of how we built Snap was modeled actually on mine and Bobby's original relationship, right?
zh: 我认为这是其中重要的一部分，但是我们构建 Snap 的很多方式实际上是模仿我和 Bobby 最初的关系，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where Bobby has a computer science, heavy duty computer science and stats background, mathematical and computational science, but he really likes design and likes thinking about building things for customers and empathizing with them.
zh: Bobby 有计算机科学、重头计算机科学和统计学背景，数学和计算科学，但他真的喜欢设计，喜欢思考为客户构建事物并与他们产生共情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have a design background, right?
zh: 我有设计背景，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: I was in the product design program back at school, which actually was part of the mechanical engineering department at the time.
zh: 我以前在学校的产品设计项目，实际上当时是机械工程系的一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: I took some computer science classes, so I sort of knew what I was talking about when it came to computer science.
zh: 我修了一些计算机科学课程，所以当谈到计算机科学时，我大概知道我在说什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we both had mutual respect for each other and our skillsets and our backgrounds and both enjoyed talking together about the types of products we wanted to create.
zh: 所以我们对彼此、我们的技能组合和我们的背景都有相互尊重，并且都喜欢一起谈论我们想要创建的产品类型。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that dialogue created a lot of innovation.
zh: 这种对话创造了很多创新。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so a lot of how we've thought about growing the company over time is like strengthening that dialogue between engineers and designers and creating an environment where folks feel comfortable throwing out really crazy ideas.
zh: 所以我们长期以来思考公司发展的方式就像加强工程师和设计师之间的对话，并创造一个环境，让人们感到舒服地提出真正疯狂的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think for the design team in particular, the thing that is probably most important is the velocity of design work.
zh: 但我认为特别是对于设计团队来说，可能最重要的是设计工作的速度。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I typically meet with our designers for a couple hours every week and we just look at work.
zh: 所以我通常每周会和我们的设计师见面几个小时，我们就看作品。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, new work every week, hundreds of ideas, I would guess, on a weekly basis that to me, if I think about where I learned how to design and build things, I have sort of an interesting combination because I went to Stanford for the product design program, which is really focused on empathy and this iterative process of understanding what people want and building prototypes and iterating, but really centered on empathy, human centered design.
zh: 我是说，每周都有新作品，每周我想有几百个想法，如果我想想我是从哪里学会设计和构建东西的，我有一个有趣的组合，因为我去了斯坦福的产品设计项目，这真的专注于同理心和理解人们想要什么以及构建原型和迭代的迭代过程，但真正以同理心为中心，以人为中心的设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then at the same time, I'd also been to art school.
zh: 同时，我也去过艺术学校。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I had studied at art center, I'd studied at Otis here in Los Angeles, both great art schools.
zh: 所以我在艺术中心学习过，我在洛杉矶的奥蒂斯学习过，两者都是很棒的艺术学校。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I was used to this grinding expectation that you're making new work all the time and then just an absolutely brutal critique process on a regular basis.
zh: 所以我习惯了这种打磨的期望，你一直在创作新作品，然后定期的绝对残酷的批评过程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so for me, as I thought about the parts of my education that I really liked, it was this combination of really developing empathy with people and the types of products that they want in their lives, but at the same time, having this really high velocity work ethic where you're not just like thinking all the time about new products, you're actually making stuff every week, all the time, and then you're talking about it as a team because that critique process is where so much of the learning sort of happens.
zh: 所以对我来说，当我想到我教育中我真正喜欢的部分时，它是真正与人培养同理心以及他们生活中想要的产品类型，但同时，拥有这种非常高速度的工作职业道德，你不仅是一直思考新产品，你实际上每周都在制作东西，一直，然后你们作为一个团队讨论它，因为那个批评过程是很多学习发生的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I would say that the velocity of work is really important because our favorite saying is, if you want to have a good idea, you have to have lots of ideas.
zh: 所以我会说工作速度真的很重要，因为我们最喜欢的一句话是，如果你想要一个好主意，你必须有很多主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's really, really important.
zh: 这真的，真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just had Keith Rabois on the podcast.
zh: 我刚让 Keith Rabois 上播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if you know this about his ...
zh: 我不知道你是否知道他的...

speaker: Unknown
en: He has many contrarian takes.
zh: 他有很多反向的观点。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is if you're building a consumer product, do not talk to customers.
zh: 一个是如果你在构建一个消费者产品，不要和客户交谈。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not only is it not useful, his advice is, its harmful because it can infiltrate your subconscious and it just doesn't lead to great things.
zh: 这不仅是有用的，他的建议是，它是有害的，因为它可以渗透到你的潜意识，它只是不会导致伟大的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your perspective on that?
zh: 你对此有什么看法？

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow, that's really interesting.
zh: 哇，那真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would say you have to talk to customers, you have to share your idea, you should share your idea as quickly as possible, as frequently as possible.
zh: 我会说你必须和客户交谈，你必须分享你的想法，你应该尽可能快地，尽可能频繁地分享你的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: It doesn't mean you need to take people's advice or feedback, but it's really important, I think, to listen.
zh: 这并不意味着你需要听取人们的建议或反馈，但我认为倾听真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think for me, it's not like the survey model of listening.
zh: 我认为对我来说，这不像那种倾听的调查模式。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think that's particularly helpful.
zh: 我不认为那特别有帮助。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think going deep and talking with someone for an hour, two hours about how do they use technology, how does it fit in their lives?
zh: 但我认为深入交谈，与人谈一两个小时，关于他们如何使用技术，它如何融入他们的生活？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do they feel about the products they're using?
zh: 他们对他们正在使用的产品感觉如何？

speaker: Unknown
en: You can learn so much.
zh: 你可以学到很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think customers are an endless source of inspiration when it comes to building products.
zh: 我认为在构建产品方面，客户是灵感的无尽来源。

speaker: Unknown
en: So pro talking to users, because I feel like you've been really good at just like, here's what it'll be and here's how it should be.
zh: 所以支持与用户交谈，因为我觉得你真的擅长，这就是它将是的样子，这就是它应该是的样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even though people may not understand this is what it should be, I trust me it'll work really well.
zh: 即使人们可能不理解这是它应该是什么样子的，相信我它会工作得非常好。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's that balance of just like, here's what I'll pay attention to, here's what I won't.
zh: 什么是这种平衡，就像这是我会注意的，这是我不会的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, let's use stories as an example, right?
zh: 好吧，让我们用故事作为例子，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: So back then, we were hearing from customers all the time, "I want to send all button.
zh: 那时，我们总是听到客户说，“我想要一个全部发送按钮。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to send an all button."
zh: 我想要一个全部发送按钮。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so annoying to select everybody on my list of friends and Snapchat.
zh: 在我的朋友列表和 Snapchat 上选择所有人真的很烦人。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you just give me a send all button, then I can blast snaps to everybody all day long and that'd be really great.
zh: 如果你只是给我一个全部发送按钮，那么我可以一整天向所有人发送 snaps，那会非常棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: And at the same time, we were talking to people about their use of social media and they're like, "Oh gosh, social media, I feel all this pressure.
zh: 同时，我们在和人们谈论他们使用社交媒体的情况，他们说，“天哪，社交媒体，我感到所有这些压力。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everything I put up there is permanent all the time.
zh: 我放在那里的一切都是永久的，一直。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's got all these likes and comments, so there's all this judgment.
zh: 它有所有的这些赞和评论，所以有所有的这些评判。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I only feel like I can put pretty and perfect things up there and I just don't like that pressure.
zh: 所以我觉得我只能把漂亮和完美的东西放在那里，我只是不喜欢那种压力。

speaker: Unknown
en: That doesn't feel good."
zh: 那感觉不好。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I actually love the way that Snapchat allows me to just express myself and share with my friends without that sort of judgment and pressure.
zh: 实际上我喜欢 Snapchat 允许我表达自己和与我的朋友分享而没有那种评判和压力的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, we thought that was really interesting.
zh: 所以，我们认为那真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we dug deeper into how people were using social media.
zh: 所以我们深入挖掘人们如何使用社交媒体。

speaker: Unknown
en: We found that one of the things they thought was really weird was that everything was in reverse chronological order in the feed.
zh: 我们发现他们认为真的很奇怪的一件事是，订阅中的所有东西都是按反时间顺序排列的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the feed, back in the day, the timeline, right?
zh: 那个订阅，在以前，那个时间线，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: When people posted photos, they would always find that the end of the birthday party appeared first in the feed and then the middle and then the beginning of the party.
zh: 当人们发布照片时，他们总是发现生日派对结束的部分出现在订阅的开头，然后是中间，然后是派对开始的部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: So everything was sort of just played in reverse in the feed, which was really strange to folks.
zh: 所以所有东西都只是在订阅中反向播放，这对人们来说真的很奇怪。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we listened to all of that and heard all of that, but then we came up with something totally new and different, which were stories that were responsive to the feedback.
zh: 所以我们听取了所有这些并听到了所有这些，但后来我们想出了完全新的和不同的东西，那就是故事，它们是对反馈的响应。

speaker: Unknown
en: They didn't create a send to all button, but they did create a way to easily share with all of your friends without spamming them all day long.
zh: 他们没有创建一个发送给所有人的按钮，但他们确实创建了一种方式，可以轻松地与你所有的朋友分享而不会整天向他们发送垃圾信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: They removed public metrics.
zh: 他们移除了公开指标。

speaker: Unknown
en: They didn't have likes and comments and things like that to reduce pressure.
zh: 他们没有赞和评论之类的东西来减少压力。

speaker: Unknown
en: They disappeared after 24 hours so that everyone could start the day fresh again the next day.
zh: 它们在 24 小时后消失，这样每个人第二天都可以重新开始新的一天。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they, maybe most importantly, were in chronological order, which is the way that people have told stories since the beginning of time.
zh: 而且它们，也许最重要的是，按时间顺序排列，这是人们从古至今讲故事的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that's an example where listening is incredibly valuable.
zh: 所以我认为这是一个倾听非常有价值的例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: All of those insights that we received just from listening to folks made a huge difference in the product design process, but we didn't build exactly what they asked for.
zh: 我们仅仅通过倾听用户获得的所有这些见解，在产品设计过程中产生了巨大的影响，但我们并没有完全按照他们要求的方式去构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: We empathized and then came up with something new.
zh: 我们产生了共情，然后提出了新的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's such a great story.
zh: 这是一个非常棒的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love hearing these stories of features that everyone's using now and how they came to be.
zh: 我喜欢听这些关于大家现在都在使用的功能以及它们是如何诞生的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Was there a moment of just like, this is the way, the story concept that you remember or was it kind of this very gradual evolution?
zh: 是否有过这样一个时刻，就是这就是方向，你记得的那个故事概念，还是说这是一种非常渐进的演变？

speaker: Unknown
en: It was iterative because in a lot of ways, some of the early ideas started more around a status update that was sort of like, how could you know what your friends on Snapchat were up to even if you weren't sending Snaps back and forth?
zh: 这是一个迭代的过程，因为在很多方面，早期的一些想法更多地是围绕一种状态更新开始的，那种想法就像是，即使你没有在 Snapchat 上来回发送 Snaps，你怎么知道你的朋友们在做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it sort of started there.
zh: 所以它大概是从那里开始的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then again, through talking to people and iterating, understanding what folks were really looking for, we ended up with stories and some of the gestures that people maybe take for granted today, being able to tap forward through stories and backwards through stories, full screen, vertical content.
zh: 然后同样地，通过与人们交谈和迭代，了解人们真正在寻找什么，我们最终做出了 Stories（故事）以及一些人们现在可能认为理所当然的手势，比如能够在 Stories 中向前点击浏览和向后点击浏览，全屏、垂直的内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: Those were all really new things at the time.
zh: 在当时，这些都是真正的新事物。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there another story of something that y'all built that just was interesting and of how it came to be?
zh: 还有另一个关于你们构建的某样东西的故事吗？那东西很有趣，以及它是如何诞生的？

speaker: Unknown
en: These stories are so interesting and people don't know these histories.
zh: 这些故事太有趣了，而且人们不知道这些历史。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of my favorite stories was around screenshot detection actually in the early days because when we first built Snapchat and we shared with people, "Oh, you could send photos that disappear."
zh: 我最喜欢的故事之一实际上是关于早期时期的截图检测，因为当我们第一次构建 Snapchat 并向人们展示时，“噢，你可以发送会消失的照片。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone said, "No, you can't.
zh: 每个人都说，“不，你不能。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can always take a screenshot.
zh: 你总是可以截个图。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you mean you can send things that disappear?
zh: 你说你可以发送会消失的东西是什么意思？

speaker: Unknown
en: That makes no sense."
zh: 这毫无意义。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we thought a lot, I remember that summer after school, we went home, we were working out at my dad's house and Bobby and I were just going back and forth and we were like ... We were having fun using the product.
zh: 所以我们思考了很多，我记得那年夏天放学后，我们回了家，在我爸爸的家里工作，Bobby 和我就来回折腾，我们就像……我们在使用这个产品时玩得很开心。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were sending photos back and forth, but people would just keep saying, "Well, it's not about disappearing photos because you can just save them.
zh: 我们来回发送照片，但人们总是说，“嗯，这不是关于会消失的照片，因为你可以直接保存它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't get it."
zh: 我不明白。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of the things we invented that at the time, we figured out a way, we realized that if you were pressing and holding to open a snap and left your finger on the screen and you took a screenshot, it would basically trigger an event on the phone essentially that made ...
zh: 我们当时发明的一件事是，我们想出了一种方法，我们意识到如果你按住打开一个 snap 并把手指留在屏幕上，然后你截了个图，它基本上会在手机上触发一个事件，使得……

speaker: Unknown
en: The phone would essentially report that your finger had lost contact with the screen.That's what the screenshot mechanism did.
zh: 手机本质上会报告你的手指已经失去了与屏幕的接触。这就是截图机制所做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So even though Apple didn't provide an API at the time to let you know that a screenshot had been taken, there was a way for us to essentially detect this touch event to learn that a screenshot had happened.
zh: 所以尽管当时 Apple 没有提供 API 来告诉你截图已经被拍摄，但对我们来说有一种方法可以本质上检测这个触摸事件来了解到截图已经发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then we would just send a notification back to the sender that, oh, the recipient had taken a screenshot.
zh: 然后我们会向发送者发送一个通知，噢，接收者已经截取了截图。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that for us in the early days really resonated with our community because they didn't mind if somebody saved one of their Snaps, they just wanted to know.
zh: 对我们来说，这在早期真正引起了我们社区的共鸣，因为他们不介意有人保存了他们的一张 Snap，他们只是想知道。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that that early invention was one of the first things that really got a lot of traction in the early days and I think helped Snapchat spread as a new way of communicating.
zh: 我认为那个早期的发明是早期真正获得大量关注的首批事情之一，我认为它帮助 Snapchat 作为一种新的交流方式进行了传播。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that is really cool.
zh: 那真的非常酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: I can't imagine Snapchat existing without you figuring that out.
zh: 我无法想象 Snapchat 能在你们没有解决那个问题的情况下存在。

speaker: Unknown
en: That feels like such a core mechanic.
zh: 那感觉就像是一个核心机制。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that is also really, I'd say famous in the PM community about Snap is you guys waited a long time to hire product managers.
zh: 另一件我认为在 PM 社区中真正可以说是关于 Snap 的有名的事情是，你们在很长一段时间内没有招聘产品经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've done all these research reports on just like how long it took the top companies to hire their PMs.
zh: 我已经做了所有这些研究报告，关于顶级公司花了多长时间招聘他们的 PM。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the number I saw is you had 200 employees before you hired your first PM.
zh: 我看到的数字是，你们有 200 名员工才招聘了你们的第一个 PM。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious if you think that was a mistake waiting that long and just your current thoughts on product managers, just like how they fit into Snap.
zh: 我很好奇你认为等了那么久是不是一个错误，以及你对产品经理目前的看法，就是关于他们如何融入 Snap 的。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of people listening to this are PMs and so I'm just curious how you feel.
zh: 很多听这个的人都是 PM，所以我只是好奇你的感受。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of my view of product management was actually driven by my view of designers and the role that designers should play.
zh: 我对产品管理的很多看法实际上是由我对设计师的看法以及设计师应该扮演的角色所驱动的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of the things that I was concerned about in the tech community, if you look at the traditional tech org structure, designers really are producing visuals.
zh: 我在科技界担心的一件事是，如果你看传统的科技组织结构，设计师真的在产出视觉效果。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're not really producing the product direction or the strategy or the vision or net new innovation.
zh: 他们并没有真正产出产品方向、战略、愿景或全新的创新。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're really responsive actually, I think in many cases to the PM saying, "This is what we're building.
zh: 他们实际上真的是响应式的，我认为在许多情况下，是对 PM 说，“这就是我们要构建的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now go make me some visuals."
zh: 现在去给我做些视觉效果。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I absolutely did not want that type of culture to take root because again, as I mentioned, we modeled a lot of the way that we develop products here at Snap on that early relationship between myself and Bobby, really this harmonious relationship between design and engineering and how important that is, that dialogue is to building great things and great products.
zh: 我绝对不希望那种类型的文化扎根，因为正如我提到的，Snap 开发产品的很多方式都是基于我和 Bobby 早期的这种关系，真的是设计之间和工程之间这种和谐的关系，以及这种对话对于构建伟大的事物和伟大的产品是多么重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think in the early days, my view was not that we don't need PMs, my view is that designers should do that work.
zh: 所以我认为在早期，我的观点不是我们不需要 PM，我的观点是设计师应该做那项工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's essentially what we would say to our designers, which was like, "Hey, if you need PM support, why don't you just do it yourself?
zh: 所以这基本上就是我们要对设计师说的话，就像，“嘿，如果你需要 PM 的支持，为什么你不自己做呢？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's the big deal?"
zh: 有什么大不了的？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that that really helped, frankly, designers play a more active role in the product development process.
zh: 我认为那真的有所帮助，坦率地说，设计师在产品开发过程中发挥了更积极的作用。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, today at a company operating at our scale, if you look at the legal requirements, our approach to trust and safety, it really does take a village to get a new product off the ground and product managers play a really important coordination role in all that in addition to synthesizing, for example, data science analysis and bringing together the working group to try to solve problems or innovate or make the product better.
zh: 现在，在今天以我们这种规模运营的公司，如果你看看法律要求，我们对信任和安全的方法，真的需要全村出动才能让一个新产品起飞，产品经理在所有这些方面发挥非常重要的协调作用，此外还需要综合，例如，数据科学分析，并将工作组聚集在一起来尝试解决问题或创新或让产品变得更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think as the organization grows and there becomes all of these different capabilities that the company didn't have in the early days, whether it's data science, you name it, product managers play a really important role in bringing all those folks to the table and making sure we deliver the right thing at the right time.
zh: 所以我认为随着组织的发展以及公司出现了所有这些早期没有的不同能力，无论是数据科学还是你说出来的任何东西，产品经理在把这些人召集到桌旁并确保我们在正确的时间交付正确的东西方面发挥了非常重要的作用。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am so excited to tell you about this season's supporting sponsor Vanta.
zh: 我非常兴奋地告诉你关于本季度的支持赞助商 Vanta 的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta helps over 15,000 companies like Cursor, Ramp, Duolingo, Snowflake, and Atlassian earn and prove trust with their customers.
zh: Vanta 帮助超过 15,000 家公司，如 Cursor、Ramp、Duolingo、Snowflake 和 Atlassian，赢得并向他们的客户证明信任。

speaker: Unknown
en: Teams are building and shipping products faster than ever thanks to AI.
zh: 得益于 AI，团队构建和发布产品的速度比以往任何时候都快。

speaker: Unknown
en: But as a result, the amount of risk being introduced into your product and your business is higher than it's ever been.
zh: 但结果是，引入到你的产品和业务中的风险量比以往任何时候都高。

speaker: Unknown
en: Every security leader that I talk to is feeling the increasing weight of protecting their organization, their business, and not to mention their customer data.
zh: 我交谈过的每一位安全领导者都感到保护其组织、其业务的重担在增加，更不用说他们的客户数据了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because things are moving so fast, they are constantly reacting, having to guess at priorities, and having to make do with outdated solutions.
zh: 因为事物发展太快，他们不得不不断做出反应，不得不猜测优先级，不得不凑合着使用过时的解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: Vanta automates compliance and risk management with over 35 security and privacy frameworks, including SOC two, ISO 27001, and HIPAA.
zh: Vanta 通过超过 35 种安全和隐私框架自动化合规和风险管理，包括 SOC two、ISO 27001 和 HIPAA。

speaker: Unknown
en: This helps companies get compliant, fast, and stay compliant.
zh: 这帮助公司快速实现合规，并保持合规。

speaker: Unknown
en: More than ever before, trust has the power to make or break your business.
zh: 与以往任何时候相比，信任更有能力成就或毁掉你的业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Learn more at vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 了解更多信息请访问 vanta.com/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as a listener of this podcast, you get $1,000 off Vanta.
zh: 作为本播客的听众，你可以获得 Vanta 1000 美元的优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's vanta.com/lenny.
zh: 网址是 vanta.com/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this interesting question that everyone's asking right now.
zh: 现在大家都在问一个有趣的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Mark Andreessen put it really well when he was on the pod.
zh: Mark Andreessen 上节目时说得很好。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's this kind of three-way standoff right now between PM's designers, engineers, where they each think they are the future and you don't need the other with AI.
zh: 现在有一种三方僵局，发生在产品经理、设计师和工程师之间，他们各自认为自己才是未来，有了 AI 就不需要其他人了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And everyone's wondering, what's the more important core skill?
zh: 大家都在想知道，什么是更重要的核心技能？

speaker: Unknown
en: I imagine your sense is now AI makes designers even more powerful.
zh: 我想象你的感觉是，AI 现在让设计师变得更强大了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so this is even more the way to approach talk about just how you think this shifts this triad with AI enabling people.
zh: 所以这也是一种探讨方式，谈谈你认为在 AI 赋能人的情况下，这种三角关系是如何转变的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, it sounds highly dysfunctional if folks are having that standoff.
zh: 嗯，如果人们陷入这种僵局，听起来非常功能失调。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is not a good thing.
zh: 这不是一件好事。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I do like that people are thinking about doing work really differently.
zh: 但我确实喜欢人们正在思考以非常不同的方式工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's imperative at this moment of time.
zh: 我认为在这个时刻这一点至关重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: The rate of change is extraordinary.
zh: 变化率是非凡的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Look, I do think designers feel vindicated in a lot of ways.
zh: 听着，我确实认为设计师在许多方面感到证明了自己是正确的。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of designers had parents who were saying, "Why aren't you studying computer science?
zh: 许多设计师的父母曾说：“你为什么不学计算机科学？”

speaker: Unknown
en: What are you going to do with this skillset drawing things?
zh: “你要用这套画图的技能做什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: This doesn't make any sense."
zh: “这毫无意义。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think today, a lot of our designers are now shipping code, which is extraordinary.
zh: 我认为在今天，我们的许多设计师现在正在发布代码，这是非同寻常的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's a really big and really meaningful change in terms of the way that the design works here at Snap and the way the creative process works.
zh: 就设计在 Snap 的工作方式以及创意过程的运作方式而言，这是一个真正巨大且真正有意义的变化。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think removing friction from the creative process and being able to go from idea to impact at the scale of almost a billion people using Snapchat is really empowering and exciting for designers who feel like these tools maybe level the playing field to some extent for creative people.
zh: 所以我认为从创意过程中消除摩擦，并能够在近 10 亿人使用 Snapchat 的规模上实现从想法到影响力的转化，对于那些觉得这些工具可能在某种程度上为创意人士公平了竞争环境的设计师来说，确实是令人振奋和兴奋的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I feel like, I don't see this happening yet, but I feel like because now it's so much easier to build, people are shipping all these okay things and it feels like design should have its moment of glory right now as a differentiator of like, "Okay, this is actually awesome."
zh: 我的感觉是，我还没看到这种情况发生，但我觉得因为现在构建变得容易多了，人们发布了所有这些平庸的东西，感觉设计现在应该大放异彩作为差异化因素，就像说，“好吧，这真的很棒。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I haven't felt that yet, and I'm curious if that's what will begin to happen.
zh: 我还没感觉到这一点，我好奇这是否会开始发生。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think for us, design actually has always operated as a bottleneck at the company, which is incredibly important.
zh: 嗯，我认为对我们来说，设计实际上在公司里一直充当着瓶颈，这非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's intentional that things need to be approved by design to ship.
zh: 事情必须经过设计批准才能发布，这是有意的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes that really annoys people and it does slow down the shipping process.
zh: 有时这真的很惹恼人们，它确实减慢了发布过程。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes it means that if you don't know how to work well with design or get your ideas in front of design, that some really innovative or cool new thing developed by a PM or developed by an engineer maybe doesn't get shipped as fast as it should.
zh: 有时这意味着，如果你不知道如何与设计良好合作或者把你的想法展示给设计，那么一些由产品经理或工程师开发的真正创新或很酷的新东西可能无法以应有的速度发布。

speaker: Unknown
en: But that bottleneck is really, really important because that's what results in a cohesive customer experience.
zh: 但这个瓶颈真的、非常重要，因为这会带来连贯的客户体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think you can see when an app has been built by teams who are responsible for different pages of the app or different parts of the experience, but there isn't really a cohesive through line.
zh: 我认为你可以看出，当一个应用是由负责应用不同页面或体验不同部分的团队构建的，但确实没有真正的连贯主线时，是什么样子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think for us at Snap, having design really actually be that bottleneck in terms of helping to manage what we're shipping and what it looks like and how it all works together, I think is really important.
zh: 我认为对于我们在 Snap 来说，让设计真正充当那个瓶颈，帮助管理我们要发布的内容、它的外观以及它们如何协同工作，我认为真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a good segue to something I wanted to ask you about, which is you're famously very in the weeds on everything that is going on, all the things that are shipping, you're very close to the pixels.
zh: 这很好地引出了我想问你的一个问题，那就是你出了名地深入参与正在发生的一切，深入参与所有正在发布的东西，你离像素非常近。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm curious how that's evolved for you over time.
zh: 我好奇这对你来说随着时间是如何演变的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you still in there looking at everything that's about to ship?
zh: 你还在那里查看所有即将发布的东西吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what's your just philosophy on founders having to be involved in, let's say, the pixels and the details of the product?
zh: 你对创始人必须参与比如说产品的像素和细节有什么哲学看法？

speaker: Unknown
en: First and foremost, yeah, this is what I love to do.
zh: 首先，是的，这就是我喜欢做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love building things.
zh: 我喜欢构建东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love making new products, services for our community.
zh: 我喜欢为我们的社区制作新产品、新服务。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is what drives me.
zh: 这就是驱动我的动力。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really, really enjoy that part of the process.
zh: 我真的、真的很喜欢过程的这一部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's not work to me.
zh: 所以对我来说这不是工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's something that I really just love doing and love doing together with our team.
zh: 这是我真正喜欢做的事情，也是我喜欢与我们的团队一起做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think for me, in addition to all the great people I get to work with here at Snap, the thing that wakes me up with a smile on my face every morning, the reason why I'm skipping to work is because I am in the weeds on what we're building and shipping to our community.
zh: 所以我认为对我来说，除了我在 Snap 有机会一起共事的优秀人员之外，每天早上让我带着微笑醒来、让我跳着去工作的原因，是因为我深入参与了我们要为社区构建和发布的东西的细节。

speaker: Unknown
en: That being said, there are always smarter, better, more talented experts working on all the different things that we have going on, but I really do like staying close to what we're creating.
zh: 话虽如此，总有更聪明、更好、更有天赋的专家在我们正在进行的所有不同事情上工作，但我确实喜欢保持贴近我们正在创造的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think it's relevant just to founders.
zh: 我认为这不仅仅与创始人相关。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think no matter what type of company you're running or your role in the company, staying close to your customer, staying close to the product and the way that it's serving your customer is the fundamentally most important thing you could possibly do.
zh: 我认为无论你运行哪种类型的公司或你在公司中的角色是什么，保持贴近你的客户，保持贴近产品以及它服务客户的方式，是你可能做的根本上最重要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think I absolutely subscribe to the old school, walk the floors, talk to your customers, get out there and stay in touch with what's actually happening with your service, with your community, with your team.
zh: 我想我完全信奉老派作风，走到现场，和你的客户交谈，走出去，与你的服务、社区和团队实际发生的情况保持联系。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that's probably the most important job that any leader can do in any company is stay close to their customers, stay close to their team.
zh: 我认为这可能是任何领导者在任何公司能做的最重要的工作，就是与客户保持密切联系，与团队保持密切联系。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you feel like, especially for a consumer and consumer social product, this is extra important to just be extremely in the weeds on everything that is shipping and say having your design team have to approve everything.
zh: 你是否觉得，特别是对于消费者和消费者社交产品而言，这一点尤为重要，就是要深入到每一个正在发布产品的细节中，并让你的设计团队必须批准所有东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you feel like that's especially important for consumer?
zh: 你觉得这对消费者产品来说特别重要吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't think it's especially important for consumer.
zh: 我并不认为这对消费者产品特别重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think it's especially important for any company that wants to deliver great products to their community.
zh: 我认为这对任何想要为其社区提供优秀产品的公司来说都特别重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: Companies have different points of strength and differentiation, but if you want to differentiate on the product experience, I think it's really important that leaders stay very close to that.
zh: 公司有不同的优势和差异化点，但如果你想通过产品体验来实现差异化，我认为领导者保持与产品的紧密联系真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything special you look for in the designers you hire knowing that they're basically running the show and just approving everything and just kind of like the right hand to Evan?
zh: 在你雇佣的设计师身上，你有没有特别看重的特质，考虑到他们基本上是在掌管大局，批准所有东西，并且就像是埃文的右手一样？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is there anything in particular you look for when you're hiring these designers?
zh: 你在雇佣这些设计师时有什么特别看重的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, there's a couple really important things.
zh: 是的，有几个真的很重要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: First of all, we make all of our hiring decisions just based off portfolio.
zh: 首先，我们所有的招聘决策都只是基于作品集做出的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's really all we want to see.
zh: 所以那真的是我们想看到的全部。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really don't care where you've worked or your level of experience.
zh: 我真的不太在乎你在哪里工作过或你的经验水平。

speaker: Unknown
en: In fact, most people join our design team right out of school, not from a big tech company or something like that.
zh: 事实上，大多数人加入我们的设计团队是刚出校门，而不是来自大型科技公司或类似的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: And really what I'm focused on is understanding the portfolio, and I want to see two things from the portfolio.
zh: 我真正关注的是理解作品集，我想从作品集中看到两样东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: The first is a really wide range, and that is the difference between art and design.
zh: 第一个是非常广的范围，这就是艺术和设计之间的区别。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there are lots of people who have a very distinct style, right?
zh: 所以很多人有一种非常独特的风格，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: They have a very distinct way of building things and creating things.
zh: 他们有一种非常独特的构建事物和创造事物的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: That really is art, right?
zh: 那真的是艺术，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: They're expressing themselves and their own personal point of view on the world, and it can be very beautiful, but it is not design.
zh: 他们在表达自己和他们对世界的个人观点，它可能非常美，但它不是设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: Design is really about empathizing with your customer, empathizing with your audience, your viewer, whoever it is, and then building something that really resonates with them.
zh: 设计真的是关于与你的客户、你的观众、你的观看者、无论任何谁产生共鸣，然后构建真正能引起他们共鸣的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that means you need to have an extraordinary amount of range.
zh: 这意味着你需要拥有极广的范围。

speaker: Unknown
en: You've got to be able to build and create things that look totally different.
zh: 你必须能够构建和创造看起来完全不同的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: So when I'm going through a portfolio, the first thing I'm looking for is like, are they building stuff that all looks the same or are they building things that look really different that have been responsive to different sets of needs?
zh: 所以当我浏览作品集时，我寻找的第一件事就是，他们是在制造看起来都一样的东西，还是在制造看起来真正不同且响应了不同需求的东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's how I know that they're a designer and not just an artist.
zh: 这就是我如何知道他们是一名设计师而不仅仅是一名艺术家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the other thing I want to understand, and typically I interview every designer that joins our team, and what I want to understand is why and the story behind the work that they created.
zh: 然后我想了解的另一件事是，通常我会面试每个加入我们团队的设计师，我想了解的是“为什么”以及他们创作作品背后的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: So typically what I'll do is just say, "Hey, pick something in your portfolio, anything you feel strongly about it."
zh: 所以通常我会做的是说，“嘿，从你的作品集中挑一个你对其有强烈感受的东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: You could love it, hate it, I don't care.
zh: 你可以爱它，恨它，我不在乎。

speaker: Unknown
en: You just have to feel strongly about it.
zh: 你只要对它有强烈的感受就行。

speaker: Unknown
en: And just tell me the story of why you made it and what you learned through that process.
zh: 然后告诉我你制作它的故事，以及你在那个过程中学到了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think pretty quickly you can understand someone's process in terms of building something or inventing something, how do they go about it?
zh: 我认为很快你就能理解某人的流程，关于构建或发明某样东西，他们是如何着手进行的？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm really trying to make sure that we have people who have different ways of doing that on our team.
zh: 我真的试图确保我们团队中有不同方式来做这件事的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the strengths of our design team has been that people come from lots of different backgrounds.
zh: 我们设计团队的一个优势一直是人们来自非常不同的背景。

speaker: Unknown
en: They might come from 3D animation or they might come from electrical engineering.
zh: 他们可能来自 3D 动画，或者可能来自电气工程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's a real strength to have folks who see the world differently and have totally different sets of skills.
zh: 拥有那些以不同方式看世界并且拥有完全不同技能组合的人，这是一个真正的优势。

speaker: Unknown
en: So as I was preparing for this chat, I talked to a bunch of people that worked at Snap over the years and I've integrated their ideas throughout already.
zh: 所以当我准备这次聊天时，我和多年来在 Snap 工作的一群人谈过，我已经整合了他们的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: But one that came up that I thought was interesting is I hear that you spent a lot of time developing young design talent and that's a big focus of yours.
zh: 但出现了一个我认为很有趣的点，我听说你花了很多时间培养年轻的设计人才，这是你的一个重点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you just talk about how you develop young design talent?
zh: 你能谈谈你是如何培养年轻设计人才的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, I think the number one way to develop young design talent is just help them make lots of stuff and get a lot of feedback from whether it's our customers or their teammates or myself directly.
zh: 嗯，我认为培养年轻设计人才的第一种方法就是帮助他们制作大量东西，并从无论是我们的客户还是他们的队友还是我本人那里获得大量反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, one of the things that we do that's different at Snap is your first day that you join the design team, you present work.
zh: 所以，我们在 Snap 做的一件不同的事情是，你加入设计团队的第一天，就要展示作品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's really, really important that the first day that you show up on the team, you're making things.
zh: 真的非常重要，在你加入团队的第一天，你就在制作东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that just sets the tone for the rest of your experience on the design team and growth on the design team because really that's what we're all about.
zh: 这为你之后在设计团队的经历和在设计团队的成长奠定了基调，因为那真的是我们所关注的全部。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think folks who can get comfortable very quickly in that velocity of making things, that's where the ego sort of melts away because when you're creating a thousand new ideas, who cares that most of them are not great ideas?
zh: 所以我认为那些能非常快地适应这种制作东西的速度的人，正是自我意识消融的地方，因为当你创造一千个新想法时，谁在乎它们中的大多数不是好主意？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's totally fine.
zh: 那完全没问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: What we want to get rid of is that sense of preciousness that people have around ideas where they feel like, "Oh, I've got this one really perfect, great idea, and if people don't love it, that means that I'm not a great designer."
zh: 我们想要摆脱的是人们对想法的那种珍视感，他们觉得“哦，我有这个真的完美、很棒的想法，如果人们不爱它，那意味着我不是一个伟大的设计师”。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, no, that's ridiculous.
zh: 就像是，不，那太荒谬了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We all need to just create as many ideas as possible that will lead us to some really great ideas hopefully over time.
zh: 我们都需要创造尽可能多的想法，这希望能随着时间的推移引导我们找到一些真正的好主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think for young designers, establishing that velocity of ideation very early on is really, really important.
zh: 所以我认为对于年轻设计师来说，非常早地建立起这种构思的速度真的、真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then the other thing we do that I think is different, although I don't know, I've never really worked anywhere else, is we don't allow designers to get stuck on specific products or verticals for very long.
zh: 然后我们做的另一件我认为不同的事情，虽然我不知道，我从未在其他地方工作过，就是我们不允许设计师长期被困在特定的产品或垂直领域上。

speaker: Unknown
en: We like to make sure people are rotating through different parts of the product to bring new ideas and fresh perspectives and also to avoid getting bored.
zh: 我们要确保人们通过产品的不同部分轮换，带来新想法和新鲜视角，也是为了避免感到无聊。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're a great designer and you're stuck designing the chat experience for three years, I mean, how boring is that?
zh: 如果你是一名优秀的设计师，却被困在设计聊天体验上长达三年，我的意思是，这有多无聊？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really exciting, I think at Snap, to be able to work across all of these highly engaged product services.
zh: 我认为在 Snap，能够跨越所有这些高参与度的产品服务工作，真的很令人兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have one of the largest maps in the world.
zh: 我们拥有世界上最大的地图之一。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have the biggest augmented reality platform.
zh: 我们拥有最大的增强现实平台。

speaker: Unknown
en: These are really exciting opportunities for designers and it's important that we are rotating them across all of these different products so they're not getting stuck.
zh: 对于设计师来说，这些都是非常令人兴奋的机会，重要的是让他们在不同产品之间轮岗，这样他们就不会陷入停滞。

speaker: Unknown
en: I had Jenny Wen on the podcast.
zh: 我请 Jenny Wen 上过播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: She was head of design for Claude and Cowork and she was like a director at Figma.
zh: 她曾是 Claude 和 Cowork 的设计负责人，也是 Figma 的总监。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then she came to Anthropic and moved to just IC design again.
zh: 然后她来到 Anthropic，又转回担任独立贡献者（IC）设计。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I asked her what's the hardest part of that move?
zh: 我问她那次转变最难的部分是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And she said it's just all the crits that she has to deal with now are just so much criticism and constant feedback.
zh: 她说，她现在必须应付的所有评审（crits）都充满了如此多的批评和持续的反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: And she forgot about that part of it.
zh: 她忘记了那部分内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's interesting that's such a core part of the way you, like the thing that people have to learn essentially and get good at.
zh: 很有趣，这正是你们方式的核心部分，本质上是人们必须学习并擅长的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, and one of the things that's so important about our design team and our design culture, one of the things that we try to do is there is no gate to showing me work every week.
zh: 嗯，关于我们的设计团队和设计文化，有一点非常重要，我们要做的事情之一就是，每周向我展示工作没有任何门槛。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there is no ... Any idea, it doesn't matter how good people think it is, how bad it is, you can bring it to that design meeting, get it on the list and share your work and your idea.
zh: 所以没有任何……任何想法，无论人们认为它有多好或多坏，你都可以把它带到设计会议上，列入清单，分享你的工作和想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that is so important because I think to your point, sometimes people over-rely on this critique process and great ideas get filtered out.
zh: 这非常重要，因为我认为针对你刚才提到的点，有时人们过度依赖这个评审流程，好主意会被过滤掉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think creating this culture on the team where you can bring anything to the design meeting, there's no filtering process, I think is just so important.
zh: 所以我认为在团队中建立这种文化，你可以把任何东西带到设计会议上，没有过滤流程，我觉得这非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: You mentioned AI, how designers are shipping code.
zh: 你提到了 AI，设计师们正在交付代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have a friend who works at a big tech company and you just said all the design team, they're required to ship like, I don't know, 10 PRs a month or something like that or a quarter, which is such a new world.
zh: 我有一个在大科技公司工作的朋友，你刚才说所有设计团队都被要求交付，我不知道，每月 10 个 PR 或一个季度之类的，这真是一个新世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: I guess one is just like, how do you think about designers shipping code?
zh: 我想其中一个问题是，你如何看待设计师交付代码？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is that like just do it if you can or is there a requirement sort of thing?
zh: 这就像如果能做就做，还是说有某种要求？

speaker: Unknown
en: And broadly, I'm just curious how AI is changing the way your team operates.
zh: 总的来说，我只是好奇 AI 是如何改变你们团队运作方式的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are just a few things that have been impactful in how your team operates that are AI driven?
zh: 在团队运作方面有哪些由 AI 驱动的影响因素？

speaker: Unknown
en: Taking a step back, it's definitely not a requirement to ship code on the design team, but I think people are so passionate and curious about learning new things.
zh: 退一步说，设计团队绝对不要求交付代码，但我认为人们对学习新事物充满热情和好奇。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, one of the things we've always tried to do on the design team is just figure it out.
zh: 我是说，我们在设计团队一直试图做的一件事就是“自己搞定”。

speaker: Unknown
en: I remember in the early days of some of the 3D graphic stuff that we were trying to do, one of our team members had never done 3D graphics before.
zh: 我记得在我们尝试做一些 3D 图形工作的早期，我们的一位团队成员以前从未做过 3D 图形。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they're like, "Okay, let's just figure it out."
zh: 他们就说，“好吧，让我们搞懂它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So they took some online tutorials and boom, we were off to the races.
zh: 所以他们看了一些在线教程，砰，我们就开始了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think the design team has always been really flexible in terms of their curiosity, learning new things.
zh: 所以我认为设计团队在好奇心和学习新事物方面一直非常灵活。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I don't think you have to push people or create a requirement or something like that for folks to want to adopt new tools and want to ship code.
zh: 所以我认为你不需要推动人们或创建某种要求来让大家想要采用新工具并想要交付代码。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the big question now that everyone and anyone thinks they can submit a PR is like how we don't break things at a billion or nearly a billion user scale, right?
zh: 我认为现在最大的问题是，既然每个人都认为自己可以提交 PR，那就是在拥有 10 亿或接近 10 亿用户的规模上，我们如何不破坏东西，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's really important.
zh: 这非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think what we've really tried to do is also develop AI tools and guardrails to make it easier for more people to participate and submit code, but also reduce the likelihood that we're going to have some sort of bug.
zh: 所以我认为我们真正试图做的，还包括开发 AI 工具和护栏（guardrails），让更多人更容易参与并提交代码，同时也减少出现某种漏洞的可能性。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we've got automated code review now.
zh: 所以我们现在有了自动代码审查。

speaker: Unknown
en: We've, I think automatically detected like close to 10,000 bugs at this point probably.
zh: 我们，我想已经自动检测到了接近 10,000 个漏洞，目前大概是这个数。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have a system at Snap where on the internal version of the app, you can shake to report your problem and agents now debug exactly what happened, what went wrong and can actually suggest a fix.
zh: 我们在 Snap 有一个系统，在应用的内部版本中，你可以摇一摇来报告你的问题，现在智能体（agents）会调试到底发生了什么，哪里出了问题，甚至可以建议修复方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think in pretty short order it'll be implementing the fix as well, which is pretty crazy.
zh: 我认为在很短的时间内，它也将实施修复，这太疯狂了。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think taking a step back, there's so much excitement around the way that AI is going to change the way that companies work.
zh: 所以我认为退一步说，对于 AI 将如何改变公司工作方式，有非常多的兴奋感。

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously software companies in particular, like ours, I think are on the cutting edge of the way that AI is changing how things get built and what gets done.
zh: 显然是软件公司，特别是像我们这样的公司，我认为处于 AI 如何改变构建方式和完成内容的前沿。

speaker: Unknown
en: We really wanted to bring some order to that chaos.
zh: 我们真的想为这种混乱带来一些秩序。

speaker: Unknown
